description: Scat

Little fawn probably born that day. It was laying next to the road in the grass and getting a close look it didn't seem to have any broken bones or any signs of impact with a vehicle. A female Tule Elk was approximately 100m away behind some brush and eyeing us intently. We went down to the parking lot for the McClure Beach and gave the rangers a call and they had received a few reports already about this fawn. About 20 minute later as we were exiting we looked for the fawn at the side of the road and didn't see it, but up on the hill we saw it with its mother! It was kind of gimpy, but I assume that's just part of growing up.

description:
163 elk grazing, fattening up for the winter. The bulls stayed far away and my budget digicam had no panoramic shot.  But I counted 163 animals in the three pictures....
This is in the big manicured meadow that's maintained for the elk, in the Fish Springs area.  On the southbound side of 395, there's a "Wildlife Viewing Area" which usually means no wildlife.  I have only seen elk there in late August and September...not earlier in the summer.  Your results may vary.
This bull Tule elk was with an additional 15+ elk cows, yearlings and calves.

description:
Friend sent photos of wild elk that were grazing in oak woodland, near Bradley, California. Photographed through moving car window. Photo credit: A. Holberg. Link to closeup photo observation of Cervus canadensis: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/140267630

Tule Elk (Cervus canadensis nannodes) Endemic in California, US: native and occurs nowhere else. Elk body and rump are pale; head, neck, and legs are dark. 
Tule Elk is a "subspecies of elk found only in California, ranging from the grasslands and marshlands of the Central Valley to the grassy hills on the coast. The subspecies name derives from the tule, a species of sedge native to freshwater marshes on which the Tule elk feeds. When the Europeans first arrived, an estimated 500,000 tule elk roamed these regions"
 
Conservation Status: Vulnerable (G5T3) (NatureServe)

INaturalist Project: California Natural Diversity Database - CNDDB
https://www.inaturalist.org/projects/california-natural-diversity-database-cnddb

Elk (Cervus canadensis) a.k.a. Wapiti, is one of the largest species within the deer family, Cervidae, The name "wapiti" is sometimes used in North America for Cervus canadensis. It originates from the Shawnee and Cree word waapiti, meaning 'white rump'. Elk range in forest and forest-edge habitat, feeding on grasses, plants, leaves, and bark. Male elk have large antlers which they shed each year. Males also engage in ritualized mating behaviors during the rut, including posturing, antler wrestling (sparring), and bugling, a loud series of vocalizations that establishes dominance over other males and attracts females. There are several subspecies.
https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/204114-Cervus-canadensis
